message.content:
PLAYER: "Scrodinger" (multiple accounts).
RATINGS: chess.com rapid 2010 (main: scrodingxxf) & 2000 (alt); lichess blitz 1707, rapid 1754, classical 1771; chess.com blitz best 1612, currently lower; bullet ~1150.
TACTICS/PUZZLES: chess.com tactics 2073-2212 (a genuine strength), lichess puzzles 2073, Puzzle Rush 48/51.
ESTIMATED FIDE OTB: ~1600-1700. Has NOT played FIDE-rated OTB yet.
REPERTOIRE — White: 1.e4 player. vs 1...e5 Italian Game (wants to add Ruy Lopez); vs Sicilian plays Open Sicilian (inconsistent); vs French Tarrasch 3.Nd2; vs Caro-Kann Advance 3.e5.
REPERTOIRE — Black: vs 1.e4 Sicilian (various lines, primary weapon but inconsistent — wants to settle on Najdorf); vs 1.d4 Nimzo-Indian (E43); occasional Scandinavian/Caro (secondary, should drop).
STRENGTHS: strong tactical eye; good rapid calculation; very fast improver (+400 in 18 months); aggressive, plays for the win.
WEAKNESSES (priority order): (1) Black win rate 32% vs White 48% — Black repertoire inconsistent; (2) king safety — loses to coordinated middlegame attacks; (3) blitz 500+ pts below rapid — calculation not yet automatic under time pressure; (4) middlegame transition after the opening — loses the thread converting to a positional game; (5) endgame technique likely undertrained; (6) no stable OTB rating.
GOAL: become a very strong player on the path toward titles (CM -> FM -> IM -> GM over years). Wants a personalized course/resource curriculum.


YOU ARE AN ADVERSARIAL FACT-CHECKER for chess resources. Category: "Improvement Methodology & OTB Prep".

Here are researched resources as JSON:
[
 {
  "name": "Pump Up Your Rating (2nd Edition)",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Axel Smith, Quality Chess / New in Chess",
  "url": "https://www.newinchess.com/pump-up-your-rating-7326",
  "cost": "EUR 29.99 (print); ~USD 30",
  "level": "1800-2400",
  "why": "This is the single best fit for Scrodinger's stated goal of a structured improvement methodology. IM Smith went 2093->2458 as an adult, mirroring Scrodinger's fast-improver trajectory (+400 in 18 months). It directly attacks his weaknesses #4 and #5: the chapter on pawn levers fixes his 'losing the thread in the middlegame transition,' and its endgame-technique section addresses his undertrained endgames. Its 'List of Mistakes' method is exactly the self-analysis discipline he needs to convert his strong tactics into full-game results.",
  "priority": "Essential",
  "time": "1 book, ~3-4 months working through it actively"
 },
 {
  "name": "The Woodpecker Method",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Axel Smith & Hans Tikkanen, Quality Chess",
  "url": "https://www.newinchess.com/the-woodpecker-method",
  "cost": "~USD 37.95 (print)",
  "level": "1500-2200",
  "why": "Targets weakness #3 head-on: his blitz is 500+ points below rapid because calculation/patterns are not yet automatic under time pressure. The Woodpecker's spaced-repetition of 1100+ puzzles converts his already-strong tactical eye (chess.com tactics 2073-2212) into instant recognition, which is precisely what closes a rapid-vs-blitz gap and reduces blunders in time trouble. It builds on a genuine strength rather than fighting a weakness from scratch.",
  "priority": "Essential",
  "time": "Two full cycles over ~6-8 weeks (intensive)"
 },
 {
  "name": "Chess for Zebras: Thinking Differently about Black and White",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Jonathan Rowson, Gambit Publications",
  "url": "https://www.gambitbooks.com/books/Chess_for_Zebras.html",
  "cost": "~USD 24-29 (print); Chessable edition also available",
  "level": "1700-2300",
  "why": "Uniquely addresses his #1 weakness: 32% win rate as Black vs 48% as White. Rowson's central thesis is literally how to think differently when playing Black and how to handle not having the initiative - exactly the mindset gap behind his color split. It also tackles why adult improvement stalls, relevant to a player consciously planning the CM->FM->IM->GM road.",
  "priority": "Essential",
  "time": "1 book, ~6-8 weeks of reflective reading"
 },
 {
  "name": "The Seven Deadly Chess Sins",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Jonathan Rowson, Gambit Publications",
  "url": "https://www.gambitbooks.com/books/The_Seven_Deadly_Chess_Sins.html",
  "cost": "~USD 24-28 (print)",
  "level": "1700-2300",
  "why": "Maps almost one-to-one onto his profile. 'Looseness' (drifting, poor concentration) explains weakness #2, losing to coordinated middlegame attacks on his king; 'Perfectionism' (time trouble) is his blitz/time-pressure problem #3; 'Wanting' (over-focus on the result) speaks to his aggressive 'play for the win' style that can backfire. A psychological diagnostic that names his exact failure modes.",
  "priority": "Recommended",
  "time": "1 book, ~4-6 weeks"
 },
 {
  "name": "Move First, Think Later: Sense and Nonsense in Improving Your Chess",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Willy Hendriks, New in Chess",
  "url": "https://www.newinchess.com/move-first-think-later",
  "cost": "~USD 24.95 (print)",
  "level": "1600-2400",
  "why": "ECF Book of the Year. Hendriks argues improvement comes from pattern exposure and concrete work rather than abstract 'thinking systems' - validating Scrodinger's pattern-and-tactics strength and steering his study plan toward what actually moves rating. Essential reading before he over-invests in rigid thinking-method routines that won't fix his real gaps (Black repertoire, king safety).",
  "priority": "Recommended",
  "time": "1 book, ~4-6 weeks"
 },
 {
  "name": "Chess Improvement: It's All in the Mindset",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Barry Hymer & Peter Wells, Crown House Publishing",
  "url": "https://www.amazon.com/Chess-Improvement-Its-all-mindset/dp/1785835025",
  "cost": "~USD 19.95 (print/Kindle)",
  "level": "All levels (methodology)",
  "why": "Built on interviews with England's elite (Adams, Howell, McShane, Sadler), it gives a growth-mindset template for the multi-year CM->FM->IM->GM journey Scrodinger has set as his goal. Directly useful for building the disciplined long-term study plan he asked for and for the resilience needed when he starts losing rated OTB games for the first time.",
  "priority": "Recommended",
  "time": "1 book, ~3-4 weeks"
 },
 {
  "name": "Perpetual Chess Improvement",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Ben Johnson (NM), New in Chess",
  "url": "https://www.newinchess.com/perpetual-chess-improvement",
  "cost": "~USD 24.95 (print); audiobook available",
  "level": "1200-2200 (adult improvers)",
  "why": "Distills hundreds of Perpetual Chess interviews into concrete advice on how to study each phase, find a coach, and use training tools - exactly the 'how to build a study plan' brief Scrodinger needs. Its dedicated sections on tournament games and speed chess speak to his rapid/blitz gap (#3) and to preparing for his first rated events.",
  "priority": "Recommended",
  "time": "1 book, ~3 weeks; pairs with ongoing podcast"
 },
 {
  "name": "Perpetual Chess Podcast - Adult Improver Series",
  "rtype": "Video Series",
  "provider": "Ben Johnson, perpetualchesspod.com",
  "url": "https://www.perpetualchesspod.com/",
  "cost": "Free (podcast)",
  "level": "All levels",
  "why": "The Adult Improver series interviews amateurs who climbed exactly the range Scrodinger is in, sharing what actually worked for game analysis, OTB transition, and study routines. As someone who has not yet played FIDE-rated chess, hearing improvers describe first-tournament nerves and time-management mistakes is the cheapest, most relevant OTB-prep he can get.",
  "priority": "Recommended",
  "time": "Ongoing, ~1 episode/week"
 },
 {
  "name": "How To Analyze Your Own Games (article series + Next Level Chess methodology)",
  "rtype": "Online Course",
  "provider": "GM Noel Studer, nextlevelchess.com",
  "url": "https://nextlevelchess.com/game-analysis1/",
  "cost": "Free (articles); paid courses ~USD 49-199",
  "level": "1600-2200",
  "why": "His weakness #4 (losing the thread after the opening) and inconsistent repertoire can only be fixed by systematic self-review. Studer's method - log expected lines, mark where prep ended, then hunt for the faulty thought process rather than just the wrong move - is the precise routine to diagnose why his Black games (32%) collapse and why his Open Sicilian/Najdorf play is inconsistent.",
  "priority": "Essential",
  "time": "Free articles in an afternoon; then ~30 min per game ongoing"
 },
 {
  "name": "Aimchess",
  "rtype": "Software/Tool",
  "provider": "Aimchess (Chess.com group)",
  "url": "https://aimchess.com/",
  "cost": "Free tier; Pro from ~USD 7.99-15/month",
  "level": "1000-2200",
  "why": "Automates the diagnostic Scrodinger needs across his accounts (scrodingxxf and alts): it scores opening accuracy, time management, endgame, and blunder prevention against same-rated peers - quantifying weaknesses #2-#5 he is currently only guessing at. Its scouting feature also lets him prep specific opponents before his first OTB events, and it will surface his Black-vs-White performance split concretely.",
  "priority": "Optional",
  "time": "Ongoing, ~15 min after each session"
 },
 {
  "name": "Your First OTB Chess Tournament: The Ultimate Beginner's Guide",
  "rtype": "Platform",
  "provider": "Chess.com (community blog/guides)",
  "url": "https://www.chess.com/blog/Tamara/your-first-otb-chess-tournament-the-ultimate-beginners-guide",
  "cost": "Free",
  "level": "First-time OTB",
  "why": "Scrodinger has NOT played FIDE-rated OTB yet, and this covers the exact gap online play leaves: handling physical clocks with increment, no easy online-style resignations, sitting-on-hands to curb impulsive moves, and managing 4-5 hour classical games. Directly preps weakness #6 (no stable OTB rating) and the time-discipline side of weakness #3.",
  "priority": "Recommended",
  "time": "1-2 hours reading before his first event"
 },
 {
  "name": "GM-RAM: Essential Grandmaster Knowledge",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Rashid Ziyatdinov, Thinkers' Press",
  "url": "https://www.amazon.com/GM-RAM-Essential-Knowledge-Rashid-Ziyatdinov/dp/0938650998",
  "cost": "~USD 25-30 (used market)",
  "level": "1800+",
  "why": "An advanced methodology pick for his titled-player ambition: it forces deep study and memorization of 256 essential middlegame and endgame positions from classic games. This builds the positional/endgame knowledge base (weaknesses #4 and #5) that a tactically strong but positionally inconsistent 2000-rapid player must acquire to push past the FM/IM ceiling.",
  "priority": "Optional",
  "time": "Long-term reference, months of deliberate study"
 }
]

For EACH resource: use WebSearch/WebFetch to confirm it genuinely exists, the author/provider is correct, it is currently available, and the price is roughly right. Correct any wrong URL/price/author/level. Set verified=true ONLY if you confirmed it real and accurately described; otherwise verified=false with a note. Default to skepticism: if you cannot confirm a specific course/book really exists, mark verified=false and confidence=low. Drop obvious hallucinations entirely. Also re-check that each "why" is genuinely personalized to Scrodinger and tighten it if weak. Reputable, well-known resources (Silman, Dvoretsky, Vukovic, Naroditsky, Saint Louis CC, established Chessable bestsellers) can be high-confidence. Return the cleaned list.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: You've hit your session limit · resets 1:50am (Asia/Calcutta)
